coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots in spectrofluorimetric analysis: A comprehensive review of synthesis, mechanisms and multifunctional applications

Tong, Zhaoyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a representative nanomaterial, have demonstrated promising applications in fluorescence analysis owing to their unique optical properties, low cytotoxicity and exceptional biocompatibility. This review systematically summarizes recent advances in synthesis strategies, detection mechanisms and applications of CQDs for sensing metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+), small molecules (e.g., biomolecules, pharmaceuticals, azo dyes) and proteins. Hybridization of CQDs with functional materials has been shown to significantly enhance their photoluminescence properties while optimizing detection sensitivity and selectivity. The article critically examines fundamental detection mechanisms, especially fluorescence quenching and further outlines design strategies for fluorescence probes based on “on-off” switching or ratio signaling. Moreover, current challenges are analyzed, such as the need for synthetic protocol standardization, in-depth exploration of heteroatom-doped CQDs, expansion of detectable analytes and rational design of fluorescence turn-on probes. Future prospects in environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics and pharmaceutical analysis are also highlighted. This comprehensive review offers critical insights to guide the rational design and application of advanced CQD-based hybrid systems.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots in spectrofluorimetric analysis: A comprehensive review of synthesis, mechanisms and multifunctional applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a representative nanomaterial, have demonstrated promising applications in fluorescence analysis owing to their unique optical properties, low cytotoxicity and exceptional biocompatibility. This review systematically summarizes recent advances in synthesis strategies, detection mechanisms and applications of CQDs for sensing metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+), small molecules (e.g., biomolecules, pharmaceuticals, azo dyes) and proteins. Hybridization of CQDs with functional materials has been shown to significantly enhance their photoluminescence properties while optimizing detection sensitivity and selectivity. The article critically examines fundamental detection mechanisms, especially fluorescence quenching and further outlines design strategies for fluorescence probes based on “on-off” switching or ratio signaling. Moreover, current challenges are analyzed, such as the need for synthetic protocol standardization, in-depth exploration of heteroatom-doped CQDs, expansion of detectable analytes and rational design of fluorescence turn-on probes. Future prospects in environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics and pharmaceutical analysis are also highlighted. This comprehensive review offers critical insights to guide the rational design and application of advanced CQD-based hybrid systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is the emission of visible light by certain substances containing endogenous fluorescence groups or coupled with fluorescent probes when excited by ultraviolet light [1]. This emitted light, known as fluorescence, originates from the absorbed energy being re-emitted in the form of light (electromagnetic wave) during the transition to the ground state. Fundamentally, fluorescence occurs due to the absorption of energy by molecules upon excitation, followed by the release of photons of lower energy during the transition from the excited state to the ground state, primarily resulting from energy release during electron transition.

The advancement of nanomaterials has led to the widespread utilization of innovative materials such as quantum dots (QDs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and zinc oxide nanoparticles. QDs exhibit narrow emission spectra, broad excitation ranges, high fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY), low limit of detection (LOD), measurable fluorescence emissions, excellent stability, high signal-to-noise ratio, the capability to precisely detect and quantify fluorescence intensity [2]. These nanostructures are composed of atoms and molecules at the nanoscale, predominantly consisting of II-VI B group elements (e.g., CdSe, CdTe, CdS, ZnSe) and III-V A group elements (e.g., InP, InAs), forming uniform or core-shell structured particles ranging from 1 to 10 nm in size, with luminescence spectra displaying size-dependent characteristics. While numerous quantum dot variants have been developed [3–5], those containing Cd-, As-, Hg- and Pb-based QDs (II-VI or III-V) are unsuitable for biological applications due to their significant toxicity arising from heavy metal ions and reactive oxygen species [6]. To circumvent this issue, it is advised to utilize cadmium-free QDs, which not only offer enhanced performance but also enable the synthesis of tunable emission spectra [7]. Among these alternatives, CQDs demonstrate superior performance compared to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, featuring attributes such as low molecular weight, minimal toxicity, straightforward surface functionalization, diverse raw material sources, cost-effectiveness, diminutive particle size, exceptional fluorescence stability, adjustable emission wavelengths and robust biocompatibility [8–11]. Presently, CQDs have emerged as highly sought-after nanomaterials within the realms of materials science and biomedical research [12–14], positioning them as one of the most promising carbon-based nanomaterials of the 21st century [15].

Since the discovery of CQDs [16], a diverse array of CQDs preparation methodologies has emerged. Broadly, the synthesis strategies for CQDs can be categorized into two principal approaches: top-down and bottom-up methodologies [17]. The top-down approach entails the extraction of minute carbon nanoscale particles from macroscopic carbon structures via physical or chemical means, followed by the subsequent separation of CQDs through filtration and washing of the resultant suspension. Techniques falling under this category comprise laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation and hydrothermal decomposition [18–25]. Conversely, the bottom-up approach predominantly involves the utilization of small molecules such as carbon-rich organic compounds or materials like graphene oxide as precursors, with subsequent polymerization and carbonization of these small molecules into CQDs via chemical reactions [26]. Techniques within this domain include hydrothermal/solvent methods, microwave synthesis, combustion methods, templating and chemical oxidation [27–31].

The structural and compositional characterization of CQDs employs a suite of analytical techniques to systematically investigate their nanoscale morphology, elemental constitution, crystalline architecture and associated optoelectronic properties [32]. Crucially, the successful synthesis of CQDs is validated through distinct photoluminescent emission under UV excitation (typically 365 nm), with this wavelength-dependent fluorescence serving as a critical diagnostic indicator of quantum confinement effects and surface passivation integrity. Critical to understanding CQDs' functionality is their thorough characterization. Optical properties are analyzed via UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, where emission wavelengths and FLQY reflect size-dependent and surface state effects [33]. Fluorescence lifetime measurements distinguish quenching mechanisms (e.g., static and dynamic), while Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy identifies surface functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH, –NH2) [34]. Advanced techniques like transmission electron microscopy (TEM) resolve particle size and morphology, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirms elemental composition and doping effects [35]. These characterization tools collectively validate CQDs' structural integrity and suitability for target applications, such as sensing and bioimaging.

Over the past decade, numerous reviews have extensively investigated the synthesis, properties and applications of CQDs in food safety and biomedical fields [36–43]. However, there remains a paucity of comprehensive summaries focusing on the quenching mechanisms of CQDs and their application landscapes in other domains. This review systematically summarizes the primary fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, it highlights recent advancements in CQD-based sensing platforms for metal ions, biomolecules, pharmaceuticals and related analytes. Additionally, the current challenges and future prospects of CQDs in spectrofluorimetric analysis are critically discussed. This work not only consolidates existing knowledge but also proposes novel insights into the rational design and application of CQDs as advanced fluorescent probes.

The emission signal types of CQDs in spectrofluorimetric analysis encompass both turn-off and turn-on signals. Typically, when CQDs selectively recognize analytes, fluorescence quenching is the predominant phenomenon observed. The mechanisms underlying this quenching can be classified into seven main categories: (1) Static quenching (Fig. 1 (a)); (2) Dynamic quenching (Fig. 1(b)); (3) Inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. 1(c)); (4) Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 1(d)); (5) Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Fig. 1(e)); (6) Dexter energy transfer (DET); (7) Surface energy transfer (SET) [44–49].

Static quenching refers to the non-radiative resonance energy transfer process that occurs between CQDs in physical contact with another fluorescently labeled molecule. In this context, the excited-state energy of the CQDs is transferred to a nearby fluorescently labeled molecule through non-radiative coupling, resulting in the CQDs being in a non-radiative excited state and consequently preventing them from emitting fluorescence. Additionally, CQDs can form non-fluorescent complexes with molecules or other compounds in the solvent due to electrostatic interactions, which also leads to fluorescence quenching. Typically, CQDs lose their fluorescence capability prior to excitation; this process is exothermic and results in a decrease in energy. As the temperature increases, the reaction may reverse, thereby reducing the quenching effect [50]. To establish that the quenching mechanism is attributable to static quenching, certain conditions must be satisfied: (a) A shift in the peaks of the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs occurs, indicating that a non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent ground-state complex is formed due to the interaction between the CQDs and the quencher upon its addition to the CQDs solution; (b) The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs remains unchanged following the addition of the quencher. Yin et al. [51] synthesized biomass CQDs using pure biomass waste from cycad leaves as the carbon source, employing a one-step hydrothermal method without any dopants or organic solvents. The sensing mechanism for Fe3+ in this context exemplified static quenching. As illustrated in Fig. 2 . (a), the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs exhibited a red shift and several alterations upon the addition of Fe3+, which indicated that Fe3+ interacted with the CQDs to form a non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent ground-state complex, resulting in static quenching [52]. Additionally, the average fluorescence lifetimes of the CQDs before and after the introduction of Fe3+ were measured at 1.02 ns and 0.91 ns, respectively (Fig. 2. (b)), demonstrating that the fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs remained largely unaffected. Therefore, it could be concluded that the addition of a high concentration of quencher (Fe3+, 2 mM/L) did not significantly shorten the fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs, thereby confirming that the quenching mechanism associated with Fe3+ was indeed static quenching [53].

Dynamic quenching, also referred to as collisional quenching, describes the process by which excited-state CQDs revert to their ground state by dissipating their excitation energy through collisions with quencher molecules. This can occur via either energy transfer or charge transfer mechanisms, leading to fluorescence quenching. Generally, elevated temperatures intensify molecular motion and increase the number of molecules participating in collisions, thereby enhancing the quenching effect. To establish that the quenching mechanism is due to dynamic quenching, specific conditions must be met: (a) There should be no significant alteration in the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs; (b) The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs must be impacted, exhibiting a noticeable reduction following the addition of the quencher. Bao et al. [54] employed citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the nitrogen source to synthesize CQDs with dual-functional determination capabilities using a one-step hydrothermal method. As illustrated in Fig. 2. (c), upon the addition of Fe3+ to the CQDs, there were no significant changes in the UV–visible absorption spectrum, and the characteristic absorption peaks remained unshifted. However, the fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs solution was significantly shortened (Fig. 2. (d)). This observation indicated that the excited-state CQDs collided with Fe3+, resulting in a loss of excitation energy through a charge transfer mechanism, thereby causing fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2. (e), an increase in temperature enhanced the quenching effect, suggesting that higher temperatures promoted molecular collisions. Thus, the mechanism governing the fluorescence quenching of CQDs upon the addition of Fe3+ was predominantly driven by dynamic quenching.

IFE refers to the phenomenon in which the surface functional groups of CQDs absorb external light and facilitate energy transfer processes. This leads to non-radiative transitions of excitons and a suppression of fluorescence emission, ultimately resulting in the fluorescence quenching of CQDs [56]. The conditions for fluorescence quenching induced by IFE are as follows: (a) Overlap between the absorption spectrum of the receptor and the excitation spectrum (primary IFE) or emission spectrum (secondary IFE) of the CQDs [57]; (b) The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs remains unaffected following the addition of the quencher. John et al. [55] developed a simple, economical, environmentally friendly, one-step method to synthesize water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) from extracts of dianthus leaves using a hydrothermal reaction for the determination of chromium ions. On the surface of N-CQDs there were many functional groups such as –OH, –NH, –CO, –CN. Due to strong interactions between chromium ions and these functional groups, chromium ions aggregated around the N-CQDs, leading to fluorescence quenching [58]. The UV–visible absorption spectra of the N-CQDs, Cr (VI) solution (20 μM) and the N-CQDs/Cr (VI) mixture (Fig. 2. (f)) revealed substantial changes in absorbance within the range of 300–600 nm. Cr (VI) exhibited three broad absorption peaks at 260, 350 and 450 nm. During the sensing process, the prepared N-CQDs-with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and an emission wavelength of 460 nm-demonstrated some overlap with the absorption peaks of Cr (VI). This fluorescence quenching aligned with the mechanism of IFE.

When the fluorescence spectrum of the CQDs overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the receptor molecule, the excitation energy from the CQDs’ fluorescence can induce the receptor molecule to emit fluorescence. Concurrently, the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs decreases. This phenomenon is known as FRET. Generally, FRET also influences the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs, which aligns with the dynamic quenching effect. Due to vibrational collisions, non-radiative energy transfer in FRET is confined to a distance of approximately 10 nm, whereas radiative energy transfer in IFE is not subject to such spatial limitations [59]. FRET is an electrodynamic phenomenon that can be effectively explained through classical physics. To establish that the quenching mechanism is indeed induced by FRET, several conditions must be satisfied: (a) The fluorescence emission spectrum of the CQDs must overlap with the absorption spectrum of the quencher [60,61]; (b) The fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs should decrease upon the introduction of the quencher [48]; (c) The CQDs and the quencher must be in close proximity to one another, typically within a range of 10 Å to 100 Å [62,63]. Khan et al. [64] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped water-soluble carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) using a straightforward one-step method involving hydrothermal treatment of l-lysine and thiourea for the determination of picric acid (PA). Fig. 3 . (a) illustrated a significant overlap between the emission spectrum of the CQDs and the absorption spectrum of PA. Furthermore, the calculated fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CQDs (Fig. 3. (b)) revealed that the average lifetime without PA was 4.78 ns, whereas the average lifetime with PA was 4.58 ns. These results indicated that the presence of PA led to a reduction in the fluorescence lifetime, thereby confirming that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N, S-CQDs in the presence of PA could be attributed to FRET.

PET refers to the process whereby external molecules effectively accept electrons from the excited state of CQDs, resulting in the transfer and dissipation of energy from the excited-state electrons, ultimately inhibiting fluorescence emission. Hong et al. [65] synthesized novel CQDs that exhibited orange fluorescence and a high FLQY of 47.1 % using 4-(diethylamino) salicylaldehyde and phosphoric acid as precursors through a rapid hydrothermal method. These CQDs demonstrated efficacy for the sensitive and selective determination of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) in complex water samples. As illustrated in Fig. 3. (c), the quenching effect of PFOS/PFOA did not alter the fluorescence lifetime of the CQDs, rendering it unreasonable to attribute the quenching solely to FRET. Furthermore, the calculated energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (EHOMO) for the CQDs were −3.93 eV and −5.91 eV, respectively (Fig. 3. (d)). In parallel, calculations employing the Dmol3 model and B3LYP functional basis set yielded ELUMO and EHOMO values for PFOS of −4.56 eV and −8.74 eV, respectively, and for PFOA of −4.38 eV and −8.81 eV, respectively. These results suggested that the photoinduced electron transfer from the LUMO of the CQDs to the LUMO of PFOS/PFOA was energetically favorable. The driving force for this electron transfer was greater for PFOS than for PFOA, which aligned with the more effective quenching of fluorescence by PFOS compared to PFOA. Consequently, in this sensing process, PET served as the primary mechanism of fluorescence quenching.

The DET mechanism operates via direct electron transfer rather than energy transduction through photons or intermediate media [48]. This implies the unmediated transfer of electrons from the donor (CQDs) to the acceptor (e.g., electrodes or target molecules) without requiring photoexcitation or auxiliary mediators. As a redox-driven process, DET necessitates matched redox potentials between CQDs and the acceptor to enable spontaneous electron transfer. Optimal electron transfer occurs when the redox potentials are closely aligned, minimizing the energy barrier; conversely, significant potential disparities may impede electron migration due to insurmountable energy barriers. Furthermore, direct physical contact or conductive interfacial linkage between CQDs and the acceptor is critical to establish an electron transfer pathway. Specifically, spatial overlap between the molecular orbitals of excited-state CQDs and adjacent acceptors allows excited-state electrons to transfer via exchange interactions, accompanied by hole filling in CQDs. This energy transfer process culminates in fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Dong et al. [67] synthesized N-CQDs through hydrothermal treatment of CA and ethylenediamine (EDA) for analyzing fluorescence quenching induced by inorganic ions. Their experimental protocol involved mixing N-CQDs with salt solutions containing diverse cations and anions. Results revealed that both cationic and anionic species could attenuate N-CQDs' fluorescence, with inorganic oxidants exhibiting more pronounced fluorescence quenching compared to other ions. Further mechanistic studies identified redox reactions between inorganic oxidants and N-CQDs as the dominant quenching pathway, consistent with DET principles.

SET predominantly occurs in systems where CQDs interface with metallic nanoparticles (e.g., Au or Ag NPs). The nanoparticle surface acts as an electron transfer mediator, capturing excited-state electrons from CQDs and channeling them to acceptors (e.g., organic molecules or electrodes) via surface conductivity, thereby suppressing radiative recombination. Concurrently, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) at the metal interface enhances localized electric fields, accelerating electron-hole pair separation in CQDs and facilitating electron transfer. These synergistic effects collectively inhibit fluorescence emission, leading to fluorescence quenching. For instance, Wang et al. [68] developed CQDs for Hg2+ analysis, demonstrating that Hg2+ binding to surface functional groups of CQDs alters their surface energy landscape. This modification promotes electron transfer from CQDs to Hg2+, effectively quenching the fluorescence through SET mechanisms.

In addition, certain carbon quantum dot sensors facilitate concentration determination through fluorescence enhancement responses to the analyte. Compared to quenching fluorescence sensors, activated fluorescence sensors exhibit a lower likelihood of generating false positive signals [69,70], although research in this area remains relatively limited. Beyond the direct enhancement of CQDs' fluorescence, researchers often construct a series of “off-on” or “on-off-on” fluorescent probes for substances that cannot interact directly with the CQDs. While the analyte may not engage in direct interactions with the CQDs, an intermediate compound can be identified. This intermediate interacts with the CQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, the analyte competes with the CQDs for the intermediate, ultimately restoring the CQDs' fluorescence and thereby enabling the indirect measurement of analyte concentration. Zhu et al. [71] synthesized iron-doped carbon quantum dots (Fe-CQDs) using coffee grounds as a precursor via a simple and environmentally friendly method. In the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), the Fe-CQDs demonstrated high peroxidase-like activity, converting the colorless 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB. The resulting ox-TMB was capable of quenching the fluorescence of Fe-CQDs through IFE, and the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) mitigated the occurrence of IFE, thereby restoring the fluorescence of the Fe-CQDs. Zhao et al. [72] prepared graphitic carbon quantum dots (G-CQDs) with favorable optical properties from grapefruit peel for detecting lead ion concentrations. A pivotal characteristic of this strategy was that G-CQDs undergo fluorescence quenching upon binding to hemoglobin. In the presence of lead ions, multifunctional magnetic beads recognized these ions and activated the enzymatic activity of lead enzymes to cleave the rA site of the lead substrate. The cleavage of the lead substrate resulted in the release of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA). Upon the removal of the magnetic beads, the ssDNA segment bound to a hairpin probe, releasing a G-rich sequence. This process led to the formation of G-quadruplexes on the surface of G-CQDs, which subsequently bound to hemoglobin, restoring the fluorescence of the hemoglobin/G-CQDs complex. Consequently, an “on-off-on” fluorescent probe was developed for the sensitive analysis of lead ions.

Additionally, ratio-type fluorescent probes represent a highly selective and accurate analysis methodology. The presence of the analyte induces alterations in the intensity of two or more emission peaks of the probe. This mechanism reduces interference from external factors through a self-calibration approach, thereby facilitating the efficient determination of a variety of substances. Duan et al. [66] synthesized CQDs using tyrosine and malic acid as precursors via an enhanced hydrothermal method. Due to the complexation between polyethyleneimine (PEI) and the CQDs, the emission intensity of the CQDs was enhanced eightfold, accompanied by a slight redshift to 445 nm. The subsequent introduction of morin, as illustrated in Fig. 3. (e), led to the quenching of the blue luminescence at 445 nm of the CQDs-PEI composite. This quenching resulted from the specific interaction between PEI and morin (morin@PEI), as well as the IFE induced by the morin@PEI assembly. Furthermore, a new yellow emission was generated at 560 nm due to the formation of morin@PEI, thereby establishing a ratio fluorescence probe for the sensitive analysis of morin.
Metal ions play a critical role in numerous biochemical processes, and the dysregulation of specific metal ion concentrations within cells can result in a variety of physiological disorders [73].

Mercury is recognized as one of the most toxic heavy metal ions, posing a significant threat to the human central nervous system and leading to severe consequences such as brain damage and mercury poisoning. Moreover, within biological systems, mercury can be transformed into even more hazardous methylmercury [74]. Consequently, precise determination of mercury concentrations is imperative. Dewangan et al. [75] successfully synthesized three fluorescent probes–N-CQDs, N/Ag-CQDs and N/Ce-CQDs-via a hydrothermal method specifically for the determination of Hg2+. The Hg2+ could interact with the nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface of N-CQDs, inducing alterations in their electronic structure and resulting in the formation of non-fluorescent complexes. This interaction led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs due to the coordination process involving nitrogen atoms. Conversely, N/Ag-CQDs facilitated the formation of an Ag–Hg alloy on their surface, which contributed to the reduction of Hg2+ to Hg, thereby diminishing the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence of N/Ce-CQDs was quenched as a result of the collective effects stemming from the synergistic interactions between Hg2+ and the surface nitrogen and oxygen atoms. Gao et al. [76] utilized sodium diethylenetriaminepentaacetate (ASDA-Na4) and m-phenylenediamine(m-PD) as precursors, employing a one-step hydrothermal method to develop N, S-CQDs. These N, S-CQDs were capable of forming non-fluorescent complexes with Hg2+, resulting in fluorescence quenching. The interaction strength between halide ions and Hg2+ surpassed that between N, S-CQDs and Hg2+. Upon the introduction of halide ions, as depicted in Fig. 4 . (a), the absorption peak of the N, S-CQDs-Hg2+ system re-emerged within the range of 400–450 nm, thus restoring the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs and establishing an “on-off” fluorescent probe for Hg2+ determination. Liao et al. [77] synthesized silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CQDs) using CA and aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTEOS) as precursors through a straightforward and cost-effective one-step hydrothermal method. They used rhodamine B (RhB) to develop a ratio-type fluorescent probe (Si-CQDs/RhB) for detecting Hg2+ by combining Si-CQDs. In the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CQDs at 451 nm gradually diminished, while the fluorescence intensity at 583 nm remained relatively stable (Fig. 4. (b)), resulting in a ratio-type fluorescent probe characterized by robust anti-interference capabilities. Chu et al. [78] prepared M-CQDs utilizing folic acid and m-PD via a hydrothermal methodology. The Hg2+ ions could bind simultaneously to multiple oxygen and nitrogen atoms present within the M-CQDs. When the M-CQDs-Hg2+ complexes aggregated, the electronic structure of M-CQDs was altered, leading to non-radiative electron-hole recombination, which resulted in fluorescence quenching. Similarly, P-CQDs fluorescent probes were synthesized through a procedure analogous to that of M-CQDs, substituting p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) for m-PD; however, the mechanism governing their fluorescence response differed. Typically, free electrons within P-CQDs were transferred to the excited state orbitals of the fluorophore, thereby diminishing the fluorescence emission of P-CQDs. Nevertheless, the strong chelation between Hg2+ and P-CQDs interrupted the internal PET, facilitating the recovery of fluorescence in P-CQDs. Behera et al. [79] employed folic acid and urea as precursors to synthesize N-CQDs for the determination of Hg2+ through a single-step high-temperature carbonization process. This phenomenon arose from the electronic transitions of CQDs from the excited state to the d-orbitals of Hg2+ [80]. Additionally, the negatively charged surface of N-CQDs contained hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine functional groups that interacted with the positively charged Hg2+, enhancing the sensitivity of their fluorescence response.

Iron ions are among the most essential metals in biological systems, significantly contributing to the synthesis of various proteins and enzymes, including hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochromes and peroxidases. However, excessive intake of iron can lead to numerous adverse health effects. Luo et al. [81] synthesized iodinated carbon quantum dots (I-CQDs) using para-iodobenzoic acid as a precursor through a solvothermal carbonization self-doping method. Most literature concerning the selective determination of Fe3+ focused on the synergistic effects of three functional groups: –COOH, –OH and –NH2 [82,83]. Ali et al. [84] employed CA as a raw material to produce highly fluorescent N-CQDs via a one-step hydrothermal process. The fluorescence quenching observed in N-CQDs was attributable to a unique direct interaction or electron energy transfer mechanism between Fe3+ and the phenolic hydroxyl groups present on the N-CQDs [85]. In the presence of Fe3+ within bare aqueous N-CQDs, electrons residing in the high excited state (S1) of the N-CQDs were compelled to transition to the partially filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ [86]. Given that the 3d orbitals of Fe3+ are half-filled, these transferred electrons could not revert to the ground state, resulting in observable annihilation of electron-hole recombination and non-radiative fluorescence quenching (static quenching). Huang et al. [87] successfully synthesized fluorescent red carbon quantum dots (r-CQDs) with a remarkable FLQY of up to 45 % by utilizing 2, 5-diaminotoluene sulfate and 4-hydroxyethylpiperazine ethane sulfonic acid as precursors through a microwave-assisted one-step hydrothermal method. The binding reaction between Fe3+ and the surface functional groups of the r-CQDs further disrupted the surface states, leading to slight aggregation and fluorescence quenching [88]. Additionally, the strong electrostatic attraction between r-CQDs and Fe3+ significantly enhanced their binding interactions, while the vacant d-orbitals in Fe3+ acted as suitable acceptor sites for photoelectrons emitted from the r-CQDs [88,89]. Liu et al. [90] synthesized biomass-based CQDs and N-CQDs using H2O2 as an oxidizing agent and PEI as a nitrogen precursor through a green hydrothermal synthesis approach. The surface groups present on the N-CQDs, such as –COOH, –CO and –NH2, introduced multiple new energy levels within the n and π∗ gap. Consequently, under specific excitation energies, effective electron transitions might occur from the new n level to the π∗ gap, with excitations being deactivated via radiative recombination, thereby producing multicolored fluorescence ranging from blue to red across different excitation wavelengths [91]. Siahcheshm et al. [92] synthesized CQDs from economically viable raw materials, including CA, urea and Tween®80, employing a microwave synthesis technique for the rapid analysis of Fe3+ and Fe2+. Iron ions coordinated with CQDs through electron transfer, forming bridges linking adjacent –OH/–COOH groups. This resulted in localized small aggregates of CQDs and non-radiative electron/hole recombination phenomena, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching of the CQDs. Distinctively, this study utilized Tween®80 as a surfactant to stabilize the particulate system by incorporating precursor molecules (CA and urea), partially carbonizing them onto the CQDs surface as passivating agents, which formed a protective thin layer that safeguarded the CQDs from impurity attachment and enhanced fluorescence intensity. Zhang et al. [93] prepared CQDs from pomelo peel using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method. The sulfur-containing groups within the CQDs coordinated with Fe3+ to form complex Fe3+-S, which resulted in a reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs [94]. Hu et al. [95] extracted the heptane-insoluble toluene-soluble (HI-TS) fraction from mid-low temperature coal tar pitch as a carbon source to prepare N-CQDs through a chemical oxidation method. The N-CQDs feature negatively charged oxygen functional groups [96], including –CO, –COOH, –OH, –NO2 and –NH2 [97,98]. These functional groups provided a substantial number of lone pair electrons and coordination possibilities [99]. N-CQDs form non-fluorescent ground-state complexes with Fe3+, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity within the solution; these CQDs have been developed into a highly sensitive fluorescent “on-off-on” switch sensor. Wang et al. [100] synthesized N, S-CQDs to serve as fluorescent probes through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis method [101]. Effective chelation and electrostatic interactions were established between the –OH, –NH2, or –COOH functional groups and Fe3+. Additionally, Fe3+ functioned as a prevalent electron acceptor due to its d orbitals being in a half-filled state, while the N, S-CQDs acted as highly efficient electron donors owing to their low electronegative surface groups. As a result, PET occurred between Fe3+ and N, S-CQDs, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs [102].

Copper is an essential trace element required by the human body, playing a critical role in cellular metabolism and mitochondrial respiration [103]. However, excessive intake of Cu2+ can lead to various diseases, including Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's syndrome [104]. Liu et al. [105] synthesized N-CQDs using CA and urea as raw materials through a hydrothermal method. The resulting N-CQDs exhibited strong fluorescence intensity, good water solubility. The interaction between Cu2+ and the amino groups on the N-CQDs resulted in the adsorption of Cu2+, which underwent chelation with the surface amino groups. This interaction suppressed radiative electronic transitions (fluorescence emission) and facilitated non-radiative electron/hole recombination, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching. Ajaj et al. [106] utilized mulberry juice as a carbon source and prepared CQDs exhibiting green photoluminescence via a hydrothermal method. The produced CQDs possessed hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surfaces, enabling Cu2+ to coordinate with the phenolic hydroxyl groups [107–109]. This coordination facilitated effective energy transfer between the two, thereby quenching fluorescence and allowing for the determination of Cu2+. Chaudhary et al. [110] recognized the abundance of carbohydrates, such as glucose (Glu), fructose, sucrose and AA, in banana juice as potential carbon precursors [111,112]. They successfully obtained highly fluorescent N, S-CQDs through a one-step hydrothermal method. The negatively charged surface of N, S-CQDs, enriched with functional groups including –OH, –COOH, CO, and possibly small amounts of C–N and C–S [113], provided an excellent platform for Cu2+ binding. This promoted the aggregation of N, S-CQDs around Cu2+, forming complexes through coordination bonds, which led to a non-radiative charge transfer process between N, S-CQDs and Cu2+, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs. Additionally, Wang et al. [114] employed Glu, EDA and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) as raw materials to prepare N, S-CQDs via a dehydrating exothermic carbonization method. Furthermore, Hu et al. [95] developed N-CQDs through a chemical oxidation method that enabled the simultaneous determination of both Fe3+ and Cu2+. Table 1 summarizes several types of CQDs utilized as fluorescent probes for the determination of metal cations.

Dopamine (DA) is a catecholamine that plays a crucial role in various biological processes across all animal species, including humans [115]. Abnormal levels of DA can significantly affect the health of the human nervous system, underscoring the importance of accurate sensing methods for DA in diagnosing physiological and neurological disorders [116]. Guo et al. [117] synthesized N-CQDs and N-AgNPs. Through physical mixing, they formed N-CQDs/N-AgNPs nanocomposites via electrostatic interactions between the N-CQDs and N-AgNPs. The initial fluorescence of N-CQDs was effectively “turned off” by N-AgNPs due to IFE [118]. At a pH of 8.0, DA oxidized to polydopamine, which interacted with water-soluble N-CQDs through π-π∗ stacking or hydrogen bonding. This interaction promoted the dissociation of N-CQDs from the N-CQDs/N-AgNPs nanocomposites, resulting in the coating of N-CQDs with polydopamine, thereby restoring the initial fluorescence of N-CQDs and terminating the IFE. Consequently, an “off-on” fluorescence sensing platform based on N-CQDs/N-AgNPs was established.

Cysteine (Cys) is one of three endogenous thiol compounds that plays a significant role in numerous physiological and pathological processes, including cell growth, enzyme regulation, gene expression and protein synthesis [119,120]. A deficiency of Cys can lead to various health issues, such as stunted growth, edematous malnutrition, hair discoloration and lethargy in children [121]. In contrast, elevated levels of Cys are closely linked to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, neural tube defects, cardiovascular diseases, Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease [122]. Zhang et al. [123] synthesized hydroxyl-rich CQDs using resorcinol as a precursor via a solvothermal method and functionalized their surfaces with acrylate to produce CQDs-O-Acryl fluorescent probes for the determination of Cys. The thiol group in Cys could participate in a conjugate addition reaction with the surface functional groups of CQDs-O-Acryl, yielding a conjugate addition product, CQDs-O-Acryl-Cys. This product further underwent cyclization to form a seven-membered lactam, releasing a strong green fluorescent species, CQDs-OH, which enhanced fluorescence and facilitated the recognition of Cys. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [93] prepared CQDs using green grapefruit peel as a raw material through a one-step hydrothermal method. Given that Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of CQDs, the stronger binding interaction between Fe3+ and L-Cys enabled the desorption of Fe3+ from the surface of CQDs, thereby constructing a dual-functional probe for the recognition of both Fe3+ and L-Cys based on an " off-on” mechanism.

AA, commonly referred to as vitamin C, is a significant antioxidant compound that serves as a vital nutrient for the human body and is abundantly found in nature [124]. It is an essential dietary supplement for the prevention of scurvy [125]; however, excessive intake can be detrimental to health rather than beneficial. Wang et al. [100] synthesized N, S-CQDs [101] as fluorescent probes through microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis. These probes utilized AA to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ [102], thereby disrupting the electron transfer dynamics and aggregation between N, S-CQDs and Fe3+, which resulted in the restoration of fluorescence intensity for the analysis of AA [126]. Zhu et al. [71] developed Fe-CQDs as fluorescent probes using coffee grounds as a precursor via a simple and environmentally friendly method, employing an “on-off-on” sensing scheme for AA. Hu et al. [95] employed N-CQDs prepared through chemical oxidation. Following the analysis of Fe3+, these N-CQDs were treated with AA. The redox reaction occurring between Fe3+ and AA led to the desorption of Fe3+ from the surface of N-CQDs, allowing them to return to their original state, thus functioning as an “off-on” fluorescent probe for the determination of AA.

Riboflavin, also known as vitamin B2 (VB2), is a water-soluble vitamin that plays a crucial role in human health [101]. Meng et al. [127] synthesized novel CQDs doped with both metal and non-metal elements, specifically zinc and chlorine (Zn/Cl-CQDs), utilizing zinc gluconate and hydrochloric acid as reaction media. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the FT-IR characterization of Zn/Cl-CQDs indicated the presence of hydrophilic functional groups (–OH and –COOH) on their surface, while VB2 contained water-soluble functional groups (–OH, –CO–NH–). These functional groups could interact through hydrogen bonding, leading to an aggregation effect that caused a shift in the absorption peaks of the system. Consequently, the fluorescence of Zn/Cl-CQDs was quenched via a static quenching mechanism.

Vitamin B12 (VB12), a complex that contains cobalt ions, enables the determination of its concentration through the measurement of these cobalt ions. Preethi et al. [128] synthesized CQDs from curry berries using a straightforward ultrasonic treatment. The strong interaction between CQDs and Co2+ resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs as the concentration of VB12 increased. Additionally, certain oxygen-containing functional groups appeared on the surface of the CQDs, allowing for the formation of complexes with Co2+, which facilitated electron transitions from the CQDs to Co2+. As a result, when CQDs form complexes with Co2+, their fluorescence was significantly diminished [129].

Cholesterol, a lipid essential to human physiology, plays a pivotal role in the construction of cell membranes as well as in the synthesis of hormones and vitamin D. Ding et al. [130] utilized CQDs and gold nanorods (AuNRs) to synthetize AuNRs-CQDs composites via Au–S bonds [131]. Utilizing both colorimetric and fluorescent methods, they developed a dual-mode probe, by cross-linking cholesterol oxidase (COD) with the AuNRs-CQDs composites, facilitating dual-signal analysis of cholesterol, termed AuNRs-CQDs-COD. In the presence of COD and oxygen, cholesterol underwent oxidative decomposition into cholest-4-en-3-one and H2O2 [132]. These resultant products impacted the stability and dispersibility of the complex system, leading to particle aggregation, which subsequently altered their UV–visible absorption spectra. Moreover, due to the etching effect of H2O2 on AuNRs, this further induced shifts in the absorption peaks of the composite system [133]. Enzyme-catalyzed substrates interacted with enzymes to generate H2O2, which then participated in electron charge transfer with the AuNRs-CQDs-COD complex, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence from this composite.

Uric acid (UA) is a nitrogenous waste product in the human body and serves as one of the final products of purine metabolism, fulfilling significant physiological functions. Zhang et al. [134] synthesized blue carbon quantum dots (b-CQDs) utilizing a one-pot solvothermal method and explored their interaction with Fe3+ for the determination of UA concentration levels. The dynamic collisions between Fe3+ and b-CQDs resulted in fluorescence quenching, whereas UA competed with b-CQDs to reduce Fe3+, which partially restored the fluorescence of the b-CQDs. This framework established an “on-off-on” fluorescence sensing platform, enabling quantitative determination of UA.

Abscisic acid (ABA) is a prevalent water-soluble β-hydroxy acid recognized for its anti-inflammatory, antibacterial and keratin-conditioning properties. However, excessive application may lead to adverse reactions, making precise analysis of ABA essential. Shi et al. [135] synthesized CQDs that emitted blue and red fluorescence using glutathione (GSH) and formamide as precursors through a solvothermal approach. Notably, the zinc-based 2-methylimidazole salt, derived from the zeolitic imidazolium framework (ZIF-8), functions as a metal-organic framework (MOF) characterized by excellent chemical stability and light absorption capabilities. Its highly porous structure featured a substantial internal surface area and uniform cavities, facilitating the distribution of introduced substances while preventing aggregation. Therefore, CQDs were integrated with ZIF-8 to produce CQDs@ZIF-8. Furthermore, by leveraging electrostatic interactions, CQDs@ZIF-8 was combined with aptamer-functionalized gold nanoparticles (Apt-AuNPs) to develop the fluorescent probe Apt-AuNPs@CQDs@ZIF-8 for the determination of ABA. Due to the specific recognition between ABA and the aptamer, ABA bound to Apt-AuNPs, which disrupted the electrostatic interactions between Apt-AuNPs and CQDs@ZIF-8. This disruption led to the aggregation of Apt-AuNPs, thereby increasing the distance between Apt-AuNPs and CQDs@ZIF-8 and hindering the energy transfer process between them. Consequently, this altered the fluorescence of the probe, allowing for sensitive determination of ABA.

Wang et al. [114] synthesized N, S-CQDs using Glu, EDA and H2SO4 via a dehydration exothermic carbonization process. The fluorescence of the N, S-CQDs was effectively quenched by Cu2+; however, upon the introduction of GSH into the N, S-CQDs/Cu2+ system, a specific binding interaction was observed between Cu2+ and GSH [136]. This coordination reaction resulted in a remarkable recovery of the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs. Consequently, the fluorescence sensor transitioned from an “off” state to an “on” state, thereby establishing an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensing platform.

Doxorubicin (DOX) is a potent anticancer agent recognized for its ability to inhibit tumor growth and promote effective dissemination [137]. However, further investigations into DOX have revealed that the drug lacks tumor specificity, with accumulating evidence indicating its considerable toxicity to non-tumor tissues, particularly cardiomyocytes [138,139]. Li et al. [140] modified the surface of hydrothermally synthesized CQDs with Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) via a two-step microwave reaction, yielding R-CQDs that exhibited dual-emission fluorescence properties suitable for detecting DOX concentrations. In comparison to simple CQDs combined with dye mixtures through electrostatic adsorption, the R-CQDs demonstrated enhanced stability, effectively preventing dye desorption and degradation, which further improved the FLQY of the CQDs. The FLQY achieved by this method reached 37.7 %, with a LOD of 0.531 μM. The large specific surface area of R-CQDs provided numerous adsorption sites, and significant interactions occurred between functional groups such as –NH2, –OH and –CO, forming hydrogen bonds with DOX [141,142]. This innovative microwave-based approach for modifying Rh 6G onto the surface of R-CQDs substantially expanded the emission spectrum range, rendering this fluorescent probe not only more sensitive and accurate for DOX determination but also promising for applications in bioimaging.

Azithromycin (AZI) is a semi-synthetic macrolide antibiotic employed in the treatment of various bacterial infections. In 2022, Zhou et al. [143] synthesized blue N-CQDs using CA as a precursor and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) along with ammonia as nitrogen sources, employing a straightforward one-step solvothermal method. The interaction between Cu2+ and AZI led to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex. Consequently, the coexistence of both substances resulted in the suppression of the fluorescence of N-CQDs.

Tetracyclines (TCs) are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics derived from actinomycetes [144], encompassing natural tetracycline derivatives such as chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, doxycycline and tetracycline [145]. These antibiotics are renowned for their remarkable bactericidal and disinfectant properties, antimicrobial efficacy and low toxicity [146]. However, their widespread use in animal feed has resulted in drug residues that can accumulate in the human body, potentially leading to gastrointestinal disorders, nephrotoxicity and adverse reactions affecting the blood or central nervous system [147,148]. Consequently, there is an urgent need for a sustainable, simple, rapid, accurate and effective analytical method for the analysis of tetracyclines in food and health monitoring contexts. In 2020, Zhao et al. [149] developed N, S-CQDs employing a microwave-assisted synthesis method. An interaction-such as hydrogen bonding-occurred between the N, S-CQDs probe and TCs, resulting in aggregation; TCs compete for the absorption of excitation light, which led to a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs probe [48]. The LOD obtained was 71 ng/mL, slightly below the maximum residue limit (MRL) for chloramphenicol in milk (0.1 μg/mL), indicating some limitations in sensitivity. In 2021, Huang et al. [150] introduced a straightforward room-temperature (RT) synthesis strategy based on a Schiff base condensation reaction, utilizing tetrafluorobenzoquinone (TFBQ) and EDA as precursors. This method achieved a FLQY of up to 39 %. Within a concentration range of 0.1–25 μM, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a positive correlation with tetracycline concentrations, with a LOD of 85 nM. In 2022, Zhu et al. [151] synthesized cyan carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) using 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (NDA) and concentrated hydrochloric acid via a one-step solvothermal method, achieving a minimum LOD of 0.15 μM. In 2023, Kundu et al. [152] took an environmentally friendly approach by synthesizing biomass-based carbon quantum dots (LP-CQDs) using lemon peel as a raw material through microwave methods.

Moxifloxacin (MOX) is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibacterial agent notable for its extensive activity against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [153]. Li et al. [154] utilized soluble starch and acetic acid buffer solution as raw materials to prepare CQDs through a hydrothermal method, resulting in blue fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light. As MOX was gradually added, the concentration of CQDs/MOX increased, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs while simultaneously causing an increase in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/MOX, indicative of energy transfer from CQDs to CQDs/MOX. The ratio I497/I435 displayed a strong linear relationship with MOX concentrations ranging from 0.33 μM to 2 μM, with an LOD of 2.59 nM.

Wu et al. [155] developed an economical and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method for the synthesis of N-CQDs using rambutan peel and lysine as raw materials. Clonixin (LNX) was employed as a quencher, utilizing a combined mechanism involving primary IFE and secondary IFE to establish a ratio fluorescence probe that effectively quenched the fluorescence of N-CQDs. The primary IFE and secondary IFE arose from the attenuation of excitation radiation and the reabsorption of emitted radiation, respectively [156,157].

Zhang et al. [158] conducted sensitive determination of the antibiotic amoxicillin (AMX) using a fluorescence probe composed of boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs). Under optimal hydrothermal conditions, B-CQDs were synthesized with boric acid serving as a modifier and CA as a carbon source. The presence of amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in the AMX molecule facilitated the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds with B-CQDs, thereby leading to the quenching of B-CQDs' fluorescence.

Kujur et al. [159] synthesized N-CQDs through a solvothermal method utilizing o-PD and urea as nitrogen precursors. Similarly, manganese dioxide nanotubes (MnO2 NTs) were prepared via a hydrothermal approach. By leveraging the exceptional fluorescence quenching capabilities of MnO2 NTs, a nanocomposite of N-CQDs and MnO2 NTs was created, wherein the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was significantly attenuated due to IFE in the presence of MnO2 NTs. The introduction of thiol compounds, specifically captopril (CAP), induced redox reactions that facilitated the decomposition of MnO2 NTs, thereby disrupting the energy transfer from electron-rich N-CQDs to electron-accepting MnO2 NTs through the generation of Mn2+, which aided in the restoration of N-CQDs' fluorescence.

Park et al. [160] developed glutathione-conjugated CQDs (GSH-CQDs) by employing EDC/NHS coupling reactions to conjugate GSH with N-CQDs, which were derived from aniline and EDA, for the determination of L-3, 4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (l-DOPA). l-DOPA serves as a precursor to the neurotransmitter DA, enhancing motor control by elevating dopamine levels in the brain; thus, it is a primary therapeutic agent for Parkinson's disease. However, prolonged administration of l-DOPA may lead to adverse effects such as hypotension, arrhythmias, nausea and altered consciousness, complicating its utility in managing Parkinson's disease [161,162]. The synthesized GSH-CQDs exhibited vibrant green fluorescence and under alkaline conditions, l-DOPA underwent oxidation to form dopachrome. Due to the presence of electron-rich thiol (–SH) groups on GSH-CQDs and the electron-deficient nature of dopachrome, covalent bonding occurred between the nucleophilic thiols of GSH-CQDs and the electrophilic dopachrome through Michael-thiol addition reactions [163–168]. This interaction resulted in the quenching of GSH-CQDs' fluorescence, attributed to the phenomenon of PET.

Pregabalin (PGB), a γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) analogue, exhibits a pharmacological profile akin to gabapentin, demonstrating anticonvulsant, analgesic and anxiolytic properties. Hamid et al. [169] successfully synthesized non-metallic and metallic ion-doped carbon quantum dots (Co-NS-CQDs) utilizing CA and methionine as non-metal dopants, alongside cobalt acetate as a metal dopant, via a one-step microwave synthesis [170]. The incorporation of the transition metal cobalt altered charge density and facilitated interactions between the graphene matrix and metal ions, enhancing the physicochemical properties and achieving greater selectivity for PGB, as well as an improved FLQY. A complex was formed between the positively charged cobalt ions and the carboxyl groups of PGB, resulting in increased specificity for the drug.

Ofloxacin (OFL) is recognized for its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and favorable oral bioavailability, rendering it one of the most commonly utilized antibacterial agents in both human and veterinary medicine [171]. Rateb et al. [172] synthesized two types of fluorescent CQDs (1, 8-CQDs and 2, 3-CQDs) by substituting 1, 8-diaminonaphthalene (1, 8-DAN) and 2, 3-diaminonaphthalene (2, 3-DAN) at the α- and β-positions of naphthalene through hydrothermal methods conducted in acetone/water solutions. Enhanced fluorescence correlating with hydrogen bonding interactions accompanied charge transfer processes between 2, 3-CQDs and OFL [173–176]. Conversely, the fluorescence dynamics of 1, 8-CQDs differed markedly, characterized by electronic energy transfer between 1, 8-CQDs and OFL. In this scenario, the excited state energy transitions from CQDs∗ to the receptor OFL, facilitated by electrostatic interactions, leading to FRET and subsequent fluorescence quenching of 1, 8-CQDs.

Magdy et al. [177] demonstrated the microwave-assisted synthesis of N-CQDs using radish leaves and urea as precursors for determination the anticancer drug nintedanib in environmental water samples. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs at 415 nm was primarily attributed to IFE, wherein the absorption spectrum of nintedanib overlapped with both the excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs. This spectral overlap facilitated photon energy absorption by nintedanib, thereby suppressing the radiative recombination of N-CQDs. Additionally, non-covalent interactions between nintedanib and N-CQDs-including π-π∗ stacking and hydrogen bonding-were identified as secondary contributors modulating the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs.

Larotrectinib (LARO) is a therapeutic agent for patients with solid tumors harboring neurotrophic receptor tyrosine kinase (NTRK) gene fusions. A. Alossaimi et al. [178] synthesized N-CQDs from orange juice and urea using a microwave-assisted method to determine LARO concentrations [179,180], monitor patients' drug levels, ensure therapeutic efficacy, and enhance treatment outcomes. When LARO molecules bind to the active sites on N-CQDs, forming non-fluorescent complexes, the fluorescence emission of CQDs is reduced.

Azo dyes are a class of synthetic dyes characterized by the presence of azo bonds (–NN–) and are widely utilized in various applications. While they are not inherently harmful to humans, the aromatic amines released during metabolic processes (such as reduction reactions) may contribute to carcinogenesis [181].

Xu et al. [182] synthesized N-CQDs using o-PD as the nitrogen source and folic acid as the carbon source through a solvothermal method. They used the mechanism of static quenching of N-CQDs by tartrazine and IFE to detect tartrazine in beverages and confectionery. Li et al. [183] observed that Amaranthus retroflexus (A. retroflexus), a species within the Amaranthaceae family, was abundant in inorganic salts, amino acids, hydrocarbons and plant fibers. Thus, the responsible development and utilization of A. retroflexus could significantly contribute to sustainable practices. They utilized A. retroflexus as a carbon source and thiourea as a sulfur source to prepare sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal method. During this process, a dehydration condensation reaction occurred between the –OH groups on the tartrazine molecule and the surface groups of S-CQDs, resulting in energy transfer. Additionally, hydrogen bonding may form between these two molecules, further facilitating energy resonance transfer. The surface of S-CQDs also contained numerous cations and anions, which engaged in electrostatic interactions with the sulfonate groups present in tartrazine molecules. Moreover, small molecules adsorbed onto the surface of CQDs could capture tartrazine molecules, thereby enhancing the efficiency of fluorescence sensing and leading to the fluorescence quenching of S-CQDs.

Cui et al. [184] developed a novel ultra-sensitive fluorescence sensing method for Ponceau 4R (NC) based on orange-emitting carbon quantum dots (OE-CQDs). By utilizing saffron T as a precursor, they synthesized OE-CQDs via a one-pot solvothermal method. Strong electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions occurred between OE-CQDs and NC, resulting in changes in the zeta potential of OE-CQDs [185]. The underlying mechanism associated with OE-CQDs involved a combination of IFE and static quenching.

Chen et al. [186] utilized a microwave-assisted approach to synthesize yellow-green fluorescent N-CQDs using CA as the carbon source, urea as the nitrogen source and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a dopant, all without the use of any surface modifiers, thereby enabling rapid synthesis for the selective determination of Eriochrome black T (EBT). Upon binding to the surface of N-CQDs, EBT formed a ground state complex that primarily acted as an electron donor during the fluorescence quenching process of N-CQDs. In its oxidized state, this complex exhibited a greater capacity for fluorescence quenching compared to its reduced state. This facilitated electron transfer to the ground state LUMO of the fluorophore located on the surface of N-CQDs, consequently hindering the exciton recombination process. Under alkaline conditions, the static complexes on the surface of N-CQDs demonstrated enhanced electron transfer properties at the ground state LUMO, resulting in more pronounced quenching effects.

Bao et al. [54] synthesized CQDs through hydrothermal methods for the sensitive analysis of Sunset Yellow (SY). The interaction between SY and the functional groups on the surface of CQDs led to the formation of a non-emissive ground state compound [187]. Additionally, CQDs could transfer energy to SY via non-radiative resonance between dipoles, which resulted in a reduction of CQDs fluorescence lifetime. Given that SY did not exhibit fluorescence emission, this interaction produced observable fluorescence quenching [188].

In addition to the aforementioned issues, the excessive use of pesticides in agriculture poses significant threats to human health and the environment [190]. Pesticides primarily composed of small molecules can also be rapidly and sensitively detected using CQDs.

Chen et al. [191] synthesized N-CQDs using CA and sodium hydroxide as precursors and integrated them with copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) to construct a ratio fluorescent sensing system (N-CQDs@CuNCs) for ultrasensitive determination of paraquat. Upon the addition of paraquat, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs at 620 nm progressively decreased with increasing paraquat concentration, while the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 425 nm remained constant.

Malathion (Mal), an organophosphate pesticide, is a widely used broad-spectrum insecticide and acaricide. It can cause human poisoning through dermal or mucosal exposure, inhalation, or accidental ingestion and contaminates water and soil in the environment [192,193]. In 2024, researchers synthesized green, stable and sustainable copper-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu–N@CQDs) using sour plum juice and copper sulfate as raw materials for Mal determination [194]. When Mal interacted with Cu–N@CQDs, temporary binding of Mal molecules to functional groups on the surface of CQDs induced fluorescence quenching. Du et al. [195] employed microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of p-PD to synthesize N-CQDs for determination trace amounts of the herbicide alachlor.

Trifluralin, a moderately persistent and toxic pre-emergent dinitroaniline herbicide, is used for selective and effective control of annual grasses and broadleaf weeds in various tree fruits, nuts, vegetables and cereal crops. Zhan et al. [196] prepared CQDs via hydrothermal treatment of fresh cherry tomatoes for trifluralin determination. Analysis using the Stern-Volmer equation confirmed that the quenching mechanism followed a static quenching process.

Chandra et al. [197] synthesized N, S-CQDs using CA and thiourea as simple precursors through a rapid and environmentally friendly one-pot hydrothermal method aimed at detecting PA. The incorporation of PA induced alterations in the zeta potential of N, S-CQDs, leading to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex due to the strong electrostatic interactions between N, S-CQDs and PA [198], which subsequently reduced the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CQDs. Additionally, Khan et al. [64] utilized a straightforward one-step hydrothermal approach, employing l-lysine and thiourea as precursors to synthesize water-soluble N, S-CQDs for the determination of PA. Table 2 presents a summary of various types of CQDs employed as fluorescent probes for the determination of small molecules.

The analysis of macromolecules predominantly centers on the identification of proteins. In the context of fluorescent protein determination, CQDs can interact with target proteins through various mechanisms. The surface of CQDs can engage in binding interactions with proteins via either covalent bonds or non-covalent interactions-such as electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. Furthermore, specific ligands or antibodies may be conjugated onto the surface of CQDs, allowing for selective recognition and binding to particular proteins. This interaction often results in alterations to the fluorescence characteristics of the CQDs, thereby enhancing protein determination capabilities. Additionally, CQDs present distinct advantages in enzyme fluorescence analysis, primarily facilitated by the construction of a “off-on” fluorescent sensing platform.

Zhang et al. [199] synthesized CQDs using o-PD and CA as precursors through hydrothermal methods, leading to the fabrication of CQDs@Cu-MOF from CQDs, copper ions and 2-methylimidazole. The carboxyl and amino groups on the CQDs bound to Cu (II), resulting in CQDs@Cu-MOF that exhibited low fluorescence emission. Upon coordination of pyrophosphate ions (PPi) with Cu (II), CQDs@Cu-MOF underwent decomposition, releasing CQDs and restoring their fluorescence. Concurrently, inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPase) catalyzed the hydrolysis of PPi to produce inorganic phosphate (Pi); Pi had minimal impact on the structural stability of CQDs@Cu-MOF, which consequently displayed weak fluorescence. Quantification of PPase activity was achieved based on variations in fluorescence intensity.

Wang et al. [100] employed microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis to produce N, S-CQDs as fluorescent probes for the analysis of various analytes, including Fe3+, AA and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [101]. ALP is a well-known phosphohydrolytic enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of AA2P into AA [200], thereby enhancing the fluorescence signal of N, S-CQDs. This development extended the applicability of this probe to the quantification of ALP activity.

Yin et al. [201] utilized o-PD, boric acid and thiourea as raw materials, employing a one-step microwave hydrothermal method to prepare boron, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (BSN-CQDs). Subsequently, they established a screening method for α-glucosidases and their inhibitors based on the combination of CQDs and vanadium oxide nanobelts. Initially, the vanadium oxide nanobelts quenched the fluorescence of CQDs through IFE; AA reduced V2O5 to V4+ via redox reactions, significantly decreasing the absorption peak of CQDs at 390 nm. Leveraging the principle that α-glucosidase catalyzed the conversion of 2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl-l-ascorbic acid (AA2G) into AA, the introduction of AA2G facilitated indirect determination of α-glucosidase concentration based on the extent of fluorescence recovery observed in the CQDs-V2O5 nanosensor. Following the addition of inhibitors (antidiabetic agents), the activity of α-glucosidase was suppressed, resulting in diminished fluorescence recovery.

Moreover, recent advancements have been made in the application of CQDs for hemoglobin (Hb) determination. Qandeel et al. [202] synthesized blue-emitting N, S-CQDs with emission wavelengths of 340 and 418 nm, utilizing renewable, cost-effective and environmentally friendly carbon sources, along with self-passivating agents such as onion and cabbage juice to facilitate hemoglobin determination. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of Hb exhibited significant overlap with both the excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CQDs, consequently quenching the fluorescence of N, S-CQDs via IFE. Table 3 provides a comprehensive summary of various types of CQDs employed as fluorescent probes for the determination of macromolecules.

Despite attaining significant milestones, the application of carbon quantum dot composite materials in spectrofluorimetric analysis confronts various challenges and areas necessitating further development:(1) Advancement of synthesis techniques: Presently employed synthesis methods for CQDs primarily adhere to a “bottom-up” strategy, with “hydrothermal” and “microwave-assisted” methodologies predominating. While these approaches offer simplicity, cost-effectiveness and rapid processing, the FLQY of resultant CQDs often remains suboptimal, constraining structural diversity and optical properties enhancement. To propel the evolution and application of CQDs, novel synthetic methodologies should be devised and conduct comprehensive investigations into the “bottom-up” synthesis approach to modulate the optical characteristics of CQDs from multifaceted perspectives. Additionally, research endeavors should accentuate the “top-down” method to surmount challenges such as elevated starting material expenses, prolonged processing durations and stringent operational prerequisites. (2) Further evolution and application of doped CQDs: In contrast to conventional singular CQDs, there exists limited exploration concerning the utilization of various dopants encompassing diverse heteroatoms (N, S, B, P, F) and metals (e.g., Zn, Mg, Ag, Cu, Au, Ga) to enhance the optical attributes of CQDs. The refinement of their performance necessitates further investigation, particularly pertaining to heavy metal-doped CQDs. Heavy metals, characterized by larger atomic radii and more unoccupied orbitals compared to common heteroatoms, can induce substantial alterations in the electronic configuration of CQDs. (3) Diversification of target analytes: Existing research on metal ion determination predominantly centers on mercury and iron ions, with limited studies focusing on other metal ions such as lead and copper. Furthermore, current research on organic molecules predominantly focuses on the determination of small molecules, while studies concerning the determination of larger biomolecules, such as proteins, remain relatively limited. Hence, it is imperative to deepen the exploration of detecting other substances to broaden the application scope of CQDs. (4) Development of fluorescence “turn-on” response and ratio-type fluorescence probes: Predominantly, CQDs sensors are predicated on fluorescence “turn-off” responses. Conversely, “turn-on” responses proffer heightened sensitivity and visual discernibility, thereby being more conducive to chemical sensing. Fabrication of ratio-type probes confers superior anti-interference capabilities and perceptual ease. (5) Enhancement of modification procedures: Physical modification of CQDs is pivotal for amplifying their photoluminescence efficacy. Currently, there exists a paucity of methodologies for modifying CQDs, coupled with a deficiency in precise control over the entire modification process when utilizing modified CQDs as sensing materials. Consequently, the formulation of additional techniques for modifying CQDs to bolster their performance assumes paramount significance. (6) Comprehensive assessment of CQDs toxicity and adoption of environmentally conscious production processes: CQDs exhibit comparatively lower toxicity than other QDs, and they are relatively eco-friendly. Nonetheless, it remains imperative to meticulously evaluate the toxicity of synthesized CQDs fluorescence probes, expand their utility in biomedicine, mitigate the risk of environmental and human contamination stemming from industrial waste treatment. In conclusion, CQDs and their composite materials, as exemplar spectrofluorimetric analysis tools, harbor extensive application potential and scope for advancement.

Substantial advancements have been achieved in the utilization of CQDs and their composite derivatives within fluorescence spectroscopic analysis, establishing a robust foundation for progressive development in this field. The strategic integration of CQDs with diverse functional materials not only amplifies their FLQY and fluorescence efficiency but also substantially broadens their operational versatility. These nanocomposites demonstrate enhanced sensitivity, exceptional selectivity and improved operational stability in the quantitative detection of various analytes, including metal ions, bioactive molecules, azo dye pollutants, pharmaceutical compounds and pesticides. This technological progress has facilitated innovative approaches to critical challenges in environmental surveillance, biomedical diagnostics and pharmacological quality control. This comprehensive review systematically examines the basic synthetic mechanisms of CQDs fabrication, with particular emphasis on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CQDs. We critically evaluate recent developments in CQD-based fluorescence detection platforms, highlighting their expanding applications across biomedical engineering, food safety assessment and related analytical domains. Through systematic analysis of N-CQDs composites and associated modification strategies, this work offers critical insights to guide future investigations into the rational design and practical implementation of advanced CQDs hybrid systems.

Yunfei Gao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiang Wang: Resources. Xihui Mu: Supervision. Bing Liu: Funding acquisition. Mingzhu Xia: Visualization, Validation. Fengyun Wang: Writing – review & editing. Zhaoyang Tong: Writing – review & editing.
